narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 50% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile shows strengths in some areas, like being a female applicant in a field that's fairly balanced, but several key academic indicators stand out as below average compared to typical applicants. For instance, your undergraduate GPA of 2.60 is lower than the average 3.21, your LSAT score of 27 trails the typical 36.70, and your family income falls into the low bracket versus the dataset's middle-range average. Additionally, your school decile for Year 1 grades is 2 out of 10—much lower than the average 5.71—while your Year 3 decile of 1 is even further below the norm of 5.53; you're also planning part-time work, which is less common than full-time enrollment among successful students. As an Asian applicant, you're in a smaller group than the mostly White dataset (only about 6% Asian), which factored in but less strongly. The model's decision was driven most by your Year 3 school decile, which had the strongest push toward predicting failure since it's so low compared to peers who passed. Next in influence was your LSAT score, again pulling the prediction toward failure due to its below-average level. Your Year 1 decile ranked third in impact, similarly contributing negatively because of its low standing. Your racial background came fourth, with a modest push toward failure given its rarity in the passing group, followed by family income, undergrad GPA, part-time status, and gender in decreasing order of influence—all nudging the odds against passing. Overall, these academic metrics, especially your later-year performance and test scores, outweighed other factors in signaling higher risk. To improve your future chances, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthen your GPA through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and consider full-time study commitments if possible. Building a stronger academic track record now could shift the model's view in your favor for the next application cycle. Hang in there—many students turn things around with targeted effort.
